skill.name: form-testing
skill.description: Test WordPress form submissions and email delivery. Validates contact forms, checks WP Mail SMTP configuration, and sends test emails for troubleshooting.
skill.summary: Test WordPress forms, SMTP settings, and email delivery from a WordPress container.
skill.author: CrazySwami

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, and filesystem patterns; several are expected for a WordPress form testing skill. Human review confirmed high-risk issues: a hardcoded external default recipient receives diagnostics, and a user-controlled recipient is interpolated into wp eval PHP code.

high_findings.title: Hardcoded External Email Recipient Receives Diagnostics
high_findings.description: The email test script defaults to admin@csrdevelopment.com and sends site URL, PHP version, and WordPress version to that recipient. This can disclose site metadata outside the user environment if the script is run without an explicit recipient.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The default recipient is explicit, and the later wp_mail call sends diagnostic site metadata to that address. This is a concrete data disclosure risk unless users always override the default.

high_findings.title: Recipient Argument Is Interpolated Into wp eval Code
high_findings.description: The script embeds the recipient argument directly inside PHP code passed to wp eval. A crafted email argument containing a quote could alter the PHP snippet executed inside the WordPress container.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recipient value is user-controlled and inserted into a quoted PHP string without escaping. The execution sink is wp eval, so the impact is arbitrary PHP execution in the target container.

medium_findings.title: SMTP Configuration Commands Can Expose Secrets
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends printing the full wp_mail_smtp option and shows an inline SMTP password field. These commands can expose SMTP credentials in terminal output, shell history, or agent logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly retrieves the full SMTP option and includes a password field in a command example. It is possible the value is a placeholder, but the workflow still encourages unsafe secret handling.

medium_findings.title: Broad Shell and Container Execution Is Required
medium_findings.description: The skill relies on Bash, docker exec, WP-CLI, curl, and wp eval to test forms and mail delivery. This is legitimate for WordPress operations, but it gives the skill high-impact access to containers and external endpoints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present and match the stated purpose of the skill. The risk is elevated access and network activity, not clear malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Email Sending Capability Needs User Control
medium_findings.description: The script and examples send live email through WordPress. This can be appropriate for testing, but repeated or misdirected runs can create unwanted email traffic or disclose test content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Live wp_mail use is explicit in both the documentation and script. The behavior is expected for this skill, so the concern is operational misuse rather than hidden malware.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Documentation
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance hits do not show real weak crypto or reconnaissance behavior. The cited lines contain a skill description and a SQL query for recent WP Mail SMTP logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines found no cryptographic algorithm use. The SQL query is local troubleshooting for recent mail logs, not host or network reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs and Device File Usage Are Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are example contact pages or official documentation links. The /dev/null usage is standard curl output suppression, not hidden file collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples and references, and /dev/null is only used to discard curl output. No evidence of covert network or filesystem access was found at these locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recipient Argument Is Interpolated Into wp eval Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The script embeds the recipient argument directly inside PHP code passed to wp eval. A crafted email argument containing a quote could alter the PHP snippet executed inside the WordPress container.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The recipient value is user-controlled and inserted into a quoted PHP string without escaping. The execution sink is wp eval, so the impact is arbitrary PHP execution in the target container.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded External Email Recipient Receives Diagnostics
dangerous_patterns.description: The email test script defaults to admin@csrdevelopment.com and sends site URL, PHP version, and WordPress version to that recipient. This can disclose site metadata outside the user environment if the script is run without an explicit recipient.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The default recipient is explicit, and the later wp_mail call sends diagnostic site metadata to that address. This is a concrete data disclosure risk unless users always override the default.

dangerous_patterns.title: SMTP Configuration Commands Can Expose Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends printing the full wp_mail_smtp option and shows an inline SMTP password field. These commands can expose SMTP credentials in terminal output, shell history, or agent logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly retrieves the full SMTP option and includes a password field in a command example. It is possible the value is a placeholder, but the workflow still encourages unsafe secret handling.

content.user_title: Test WordPress Forms and Email Delivery
content.value_statement: WordPress form failures are hard to diagnose when submission, SMTP, and email receipt all interact. This skill guides targeted checks for forms, WP Mail SMTP, and delivery results.
actual_capabilities: Checks whether WP Mail SMTP is active in a WordPress container.
Reads selected SMTP status details through WP-CLI commands.
Sends WordPress test emails with wp_mail for delivery verification.
Provides curl examples for contact form submission testing.
Reviews nonce, validation, redirect, and email receipt checklist items.
Documents common causes for form and email delivery failures.
limitations: Requires access to the target WordPress container and WP-CLI.
Can send real email, so recipients must be selected carefully.
Does not verify inbox delivery unless the user confirms receipt.
Examples are tailored to WordPress and may need site-specific changes.

use_cases.title: Verify a Contact Form Before Launch
use_cases.description: Run submission, redirect, nonce, and email checks before a WordPress site goes live.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot SMTP Delivery Problems
use_cases.description: Check plugin status, SMTP settings, and a live wp_mail test when messages are missing.

use_cases.title: Document Form QA Results
use_cases.description: Create a clear audit record covering fields, validation, email receipt, and security checks.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Email Delivery
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a local WordPress container.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the form-testing skill to check email delivery for my WordPress container named wordpress. Send the test only to my provided recipient address.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Contact Form
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of a contact page.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the form-testing skill to audit my WordPress contact form. Check field validation, nonce handling, redirect behavior, and email delivery.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot SMTP Settings
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate diagnosis of failed email delivery.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the form-testing skill to inspect WP Mail SMTP status and identify likely causes for failed WordPress email delivery. Do not print passwords or secret values.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Full Form QA Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the form-testing skill to create a full QA report for contact and inquiry forms. Include delivery, validation, security, and recommended fixes.

output_examples.input: Check whether WordPress can send a test email from the container.
output_examples.output: A short delivery result showing plugin status, selected SMTP details, send status, recipient, and next troubleshooting steps.

output_examples.input: Audit the contact form on a WordPress contact page.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering form fields, validation behavior, nonce protection, redirect result, and email receipt confirmation.

output_examples.input: Explain why a form submits but no email arrives.
output_examples.output: A prioritized diagnosis covering SMTP plugin status, mailer configuration, PHP mail fallback, spam filtering, and debug log review.

best_practices: Always pass an explicit test recipient that belongs to the site owner or tester.
Redact SMTP passwords and tokens from terminal output, logs, and reports.
Run tests in staging before sending messages from production systems.
anti_patterns: Do not run the email test with the default recipient unchanged.
Do not paste full SMTP option output into shared tickets or chat logs.
Do not use live customer addresses for repeated test submissions.

faq.question: What does this skill test?
faq.answer: It tests WordPress form submissions, WP Mail SMTP status, wp_mail delivery, redirects, validation, and basic form security checks.

faq.question: Does it send real email?
faq.answer: Yes. The provided script and examples can send live WordPress email, so use a controlled test recipient.

faq.question: Can it confirm inbox receipt automatically?
faq.answer: No. It can send the message and report success from WordPress, but the user must confirm inbox receipt.

faq.question: Does it require Docker?
faq.answer: The included commands assume a WordPress Docker container with WP-CLI access.

faq.question: Can it expose SMTP secrets?
faq.answer: Some documented commands can print full SMTP settings. Users should redact passwords and avoid sharing raw output.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish as written?
faq.answer: No. The hardcoded default recipient and unsafe wp eval interpolation should be fixed before publication.
